title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for a heritage food project. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Leila, The Recipe Collector
Leila joined the heritage food project to collect family recipes from residents who had moved to the town from different countries and regions. [47]She learned to begin each interview by asking about the kitchen where the dish was first made, rather than asking for ingredients immediately.[/47] That question usually unlocked unexpected memories of relatives, weather and celebrations. Her background as a language teacher helped her slow conversations down without making speakers feel corrected. [48]The hardest moment came when two sisters disagreed fiercely about whether their grandmother had used lemon or vinegar in the original recipe.[/48] Leila decided to record both versions and note the argument as part of the story. She was delighted when younger relatives asked for copies after the interviews. Leila now believes recipes are not instructions fixed forever, but living records of migration, memory and compromise. She keeps a notebook full of smells she cannot translate easily.
texts.B:
### Peter, The Community Kitchen Cook
Peter volunteered to cook sample dishes for the project’s monthly tasting evenings in the library hall. [49]His experience running a café helped him scale tiny handwritten recipes up for forty people without losing their familiar flavour or texture.[/49] He had expected the work to be relaxed, but every cook proudly guarded small details fiercely. One man insisted the onions had to be sliced, never chopped. [50]Peter’s biggest mistake was replacing an unavailable spice with something similar, only to be told that the smell had changed the whole dish.[/50] After that embarrassing lesson he phoned contributors before making substitutions. He enjoyed the noise of people recognising food from childhood and then arguing cheerfully about the best version. Peter says the crowded tastings proved that authenticity is not a museum label; it is a conversation around a crowded table. He now writes notes in pencil, because every recipe changes slightly.
texts.C:
### Mina, The Illustration Volunteer
Mina designed illustrations for the recipe booklet after the organisers saw her drawings on a café noticeboard near the station. [51]She chose to draw hands folding, stirring or sprinkling, because faces would have made some contributors feel exposed.[/51] Her usual paid work was children’s book illustration, so she enjoyed making food look warm without becoming cartoonish. [52]The greatest challenge was drawing dishes she had never tasted from descriptions that included no measurements and many family jokes and gestures.[/52] She asked contributors to send photographs of bowls, pans and serving cloths. Mina was unexpectedly touched when one elderly man cried after seeing his mother’s soup pot drawn accurately on paper. The booklet took longer than expected because she redrew several finished pages after careful community feedback. Mina says illustration can show care, especially when a written recipe cannot capture texture, steam or the shape of someone’s careful hand.
texts.D:
### Oscar, The Market Day Organiser
Oscar organised the project’s final market day, where residents sold small portions of dishes from the newly printed booklet. [53]He first became involved because his daughter’s class was studying local migration stories and needed volunteers for a school display.[/53] He knew nothing about food history, but he was good at practical schedules and floor plans. [54]His main difficulty was arranging stalls so that strong smells did not overwhelm the quieter herbal tea and cake stands.[/54] He walked around the hall repeatedly with coloured sticky notes, imagining the likely route visitors would take. [55]He was surprised by how many people came not to eat, but to tell stories about meals they missed.[/55] Oscar later carefully created a waiting list for contributors to future community events. [56]He now wants each future market day to include a recording table, so spoken memories are not lost after the plates are cleared away.[/56]

questions.question: starts interviews by asking about the place where a dish was first prepared?
questions.explanation: Leila begins by asking about the kitchen where the dish was first made.

questions.question: had to handle a disagreement between relatives about a recipe detail?
questions.explanation: Leila recorded both versions after two sisters argued about lemon or vinegar.

questions.question: used professional cooking experience to make small recipes work for a large group?
questions.explanation: Peter’s café experience helped him scale handwritten recipes up for forty people.

questions.question: learned that changing one ingredient could alter a dish too much?
questions.explanation: Peter replaced an unavailable spice and was told the smell changed the whole dish.

questions.question: avoided drawing faces to protect contributors’ privacy?
questions.explanation: Mina drew hands instead of faces because some contributors might feel exposed.

questions.question: struggled to illustrate food based on vague descriptions and jokes?
questions.explanation: Mina had to draw dishes she had never tasted from imprecise descriptions.

questions.question: joined through a school project involving their child?
questions.explanation: Oscar became involved because his daughter’s class needed volunteers for a display.

questions.question: had to position stalls carefully because of competing smells?
questions.explanation: Oscar arranged stalls so strong smells did not overwhelm quieter stands.

questions.question: was surprised that visitors mainly wanted to share memories rather than eat?
questions.explanation: Oscar found many people came to tell stories about meals they missed.

questions.question: wants future events to collect spoken memories as well as serve food?
questions.explanation: Oscar wants a recording table so memories are not lost after the market day.
